segments.s403-g33: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). بر is the preposition 'on/upon' placed before the noun phrase (classical preposition + object order). نامه‌بر is a compound noun 'letter‑carrier / courier'. مهر here is the physical seal/stamp (not 'love') attached to official letters; اسفندیار is the proper name naming whose seal it is. The whole segment functions as the subordinate temporal phrase that the next verb completes.
segments.s403-g34: نهادند is the 3rd person plural past of نهادن 'to place/set' (they placed). و simply links the coordinated action. جستند here is used in the sense 'sought/asked for' (past, 3rd pl.), not the literal 'leapt'. چَندی precedes the noun to mean 'several / a few'. سوار is the plural/collective noun 'riders/horsemen'; word order is verb + quantifier + noun, typical of classical narrative compression.
segments.s403-g35: هیونان is the plural of هیون 'beasts/monsters' (here literal animals). کفک‌افگن is a descriptive compound 'foam‑throwing / foaming' modifying the beasts; و links the next adjective. تیزرو is an adjective 'swift' (literally 'sharp‑running') also modifying the animals. Adjectives follow the noun in this compact descriptive phrase.
segments.s403-g36: به marks direction/recipient 'to / into' (به ایران = 'to Iran'). فرستاد is the 3rd person singular past of فرستادن 'to send/dispatch' (they/he sent). سالار is the agent/title 'commander/chief' and نو 'new' modifies سالار; together they indicate the beasts were sent 'with a new commander' or 'under a new commander.'
segments.s403-g37: بماند is a past/narrative form meaning 'remained / stayed / followed on' (here 'he remained/continued' in the sense of pursuing/following up). از پی is a closed prepositional expression 'after / in pursuit of' and governs the following object. پاسخ‌نامه is the direct object 'the reply/response letter' and را is the definite object marker emphasizing that object. The whole line says someone continued in pursuit of the reply‑letter.
segments.s403-g38: بکشت is an archaic past form functioning as a transitive past verb 'slew / killed' (equivalent to کشت). آتش is the grammatical subject 'the fire' and مرد is the patient 'man'; بدکامه ('ill‑willed / ill‑disposed') qualifies مرد. The final را is the object marker attached to the man. Word order here places the verb before subject and object in compressed epic style: verb (بکشت) + subject (آتش) + object with را (مرد بدکامه را).
segments.s403-g39: بسی here means 'much / much effect' and with برنیامد ('did not come forth / did not avail') yields the idiom 'بسی برنیامد' = 'it availed little' or 'it did not do much'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that / until', and پاسخ رسید is the clause 'the answer arrived' (رسید = past 'arrived/was received'). The whole segment means that little was achieved until the reply came.
segments.s403-g40: یکی here functions as 'one / someone' (indefinite subject). نامه is 'letter'; the following two بد + بند form a descriptive compound meaning roughly 'badly‑bound / ill‑sealed' (بند = binding/band/fetter used for the fastening). بد (second) intensifies the ill quality. را is the object marker attached to the letter. کلید is 'key' (here understood as 'with a key / using a key'—the noun stands for the instrument used to open the letter). The terse sequence thus says 'someone (with) a key opened the badly‑bound letter.'
segments.s403-g81: سوار هیونان is a noun phrase meaning “riders (سوار) of the Hyunan (هیونان)”; in classical/poetic Persian the expected ezāfe linking is often omitted, so the two nouns stand adjacent. چو here functions as a temporal conjunction “when” introducing the clause; it precedes the verb phrase (typical in poetry). باز is an adverb “again/back,” placed immediately before the verb to give the sense “returned.” آمدند is the third-person plural past/past-perfective verb “they came/arrived,” agreeing with the plural subject “riders.”
segments.s403-g82: به نزد is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/toward the presence of” (به + نزد together form the idiom 'to the presence of'); تهمتن is a proper epithet/name (Tahmatan, “the mighty one”) serving as the object of that phrase. فراز is an adverbial element meaning “up/on high/forward” placed before the verb; together فراز آمدند yields the sense “they came up/advanced/approached.” آمدند is again the third-person plural past verb “they came.” Word order (Prepositional phrase + adverb + verb) is normal for narrative Persian and is slightly elevated/poetic here.
segments.s403-g1: دبیر جهاندیده را پیش خواند is a past‑tense clause: دبیر جهاندیده is a noun phrase (دبیر 'scribe' modified by the adjective/epithet جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise'); را is the definite object marker attached to that phrase; پیش‌خواند is the verb in 3rd person past meaning 'called/summoned forward' (پیش here = 'before/in the presence of'). The implied subject ('he') is omitted, as is common in Persian.
segments.s403-g2: ازان چاره و چنگ چندی براند: ازان (archaic contraction of از آن) = 'from that/thereupon'; چاره و چنگ are two coordinated nouns ('the device/plan and the harp'); چندی here is an adverb 'for a short while/a little'; براند is a past 3rd‑person verb (rāndan) used poetically — here meaning 'struck/played' or 'drove forth' the sound of the harp. The preposition/particle ازان scopes over the coordinated nouns.
segments.s403-g3: بر تخت بنشست فرخ دبیر places the locative phrase 'بر تخت' ('on the throne') before the verb بنشست ('sat down', past 3rd person). The nominal phrase فرخ دبیر follows as subject ('the fortunate scribe' — فرخ as an epithet/adjective before دبیر). This order (locative + verb + subject) is a stylistic/poetic arrangement rather than strict modern SOV.
segments.s403-g4: قلم خواست و قرطاس و مشک و عبیر: قلم خواست is a simple past clause ('he wanted/asked for a pen'), with خواست as the past of خواستن. The following items are a coordinated list introduced by و: 'and paper and musk and fragrance/ambergris' — all objects of the wanting/asking implied by قلم خواست.
segments.s403-g5: نخستین که = 'when first' or 'as soon as'; که is the subordinating conjunction introducing the clause. نوک قلم شد سیاه is a stative/result clause: نوک قلم 'the tip of the pen' is the subject, شد (past of شدن) = 'became', and سیاه is the predicate adjective 'black'. The whole segment means 'when the pen's tip first turned black.'
segments.s403-g6: گرفت آفرین بر خداوند ماه uses گرفت ('took/held' in literal sense) as a light verb collocated with the noun آفرین ('acclamation/praise'), so the phrase means 'he uttered/cried out praise.' بر + noun expresses the target of the praise: بر خداوند ماه = 'upon/to the Lord Moon' (خداوند here is an honorific 'lord').
segments.s403-g7: خداوند کیوان و ناهید و هور is a coordinated list of names/titles introduced by the honorific خداوند ('lord'). The construction places خداوند before the first item and then lists other proper names (کیوان, ناهید) or epithets (هور) joined by و. Grammatically it's a simple enumeration.
segments.s403-g8: خداوند پیل و خداوند مور repeats the honorific خداوند before each noun in the second pair: 'the lord of the elephant and the lord of the ant.' The repetition gives rhythmic/poetic emphasis; پیل 'elephant' and مور 'ant' are contrasted as large and small.
segments.s403-g17: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). برگشایم is a first‑person present-subjunctive/optative ('I may / should reveal' or 'if I were to reveal') placed before its object for poetic effect. سراسر سخن is a noun phrase 'the entire speech/tale' (sراسر = entire modifying سخن = speech/words).
segments.s403-g18: سر مرد is a nominal phrase 'the man's head/condition' (poetic omission of ezāfe is common); نو گردد is a third‑person singular present‑subjunctive/optative of گشتن used as 'become/turn into' and placed at the end as normal in Persian. از غم کهن is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of ancient sorrow' giving the cause or source of that change.
segments.s403-g19: چه دستور باشد is an interrogative phrase 'what command may there be' with باشد as a subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' used to ask politely about a possible order. مرا را / مرا (here shown as م + را) is the first‑person object 'for me' and شهریار at the end is a vocative 'O king' addressing the ruler.
segments.s403-g20: بخوانم is first‑person present‑subjunctive 'shall I read/recite' used to ask permission or offer to act. برو here is the simple imperative/particle 'go' (its exact pragmatic role is poetically compressed—either an exhortation or a linking particle), and نامه کارزار is a noun‑noun combination 'the letter of the campaign/war' where نامه is the object and کارزار specifies its subject.
segments.s403-g21: به دیدار او is a prepositional phrase 'on meeting him / at his sight'. شاد و خرم are two coordinate adjectives 'happy and merry/joyful' that function as predicative complements, and شوم is first‑person present‑subjunctive/optative 'I may become / I shall become', placed at the end as the main verb.
segments.s403-g22: ازین is the poetic form of 'از این' ('from this'); رنج دیرینه is a noun phrase 'this long‑standing / ancient suffering'. بی‌غم شوم is 'I become without sorrow / I become free of grief', again with شوم as first‑person optative at the clause end expressing the hoped‑for result.
segments.s403-g23: وزان is a contraction of و + زان ('and from that' / 'and from those'), linking this clause to the previous; چاره‌هایی is the plural 'remedies/solutions' and که من ساختم is a relative clause 'which I made/composed', with ساختم a simple past first‑person verb placed at the end of the relative clause.
segments.s403-g24: که تا here introduces purpose/result: the first که plus تا functions as 'so that / in order that'. دل ز کینه is the object phrase 'the heart from hatred' (ز marking separation/removal), and بپرداختم is past‑tense first person 'I dealt with / I removed' (here 'I rid/cleansed'), completing the purpose clause and placed at the very end as is standard in Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s403-g25: به is the locative/prepositional marker 'at/in'; رویین دژ is a compound adjective + noun ('iron fortress') with the adjective before the noun in the classical word order. ارجاسپ و کهرم are coordinated names/appositions following the location; نماند is the past tense (3rd sg.) of ماندن used impersonally ('there remained' / 'was left') and carries a negative sense here that will be completed by the following clause (جز …).
segments.s403-g26: جز از introduces the exception ('except for' / 'nothing remained except'), where از is the 'from/of' cause/material preposition. The three nouns مویه، درد، ماتم are a coordinated list ('wailing, sorrow/pain, mourning') that form the sole remainder; نماند repeats the past impersonal verb 'remained' so the whole phrase reads idiomatically 'there remained nothing but…'.
segments.s403-g27: کسی را is the (indefinite) object with the definite object marker را; ندادم is the 1st person past negative of دادن ('I did not give'). به جان زینهار is a noun phrase 'a warning/admonition for (one's) life' — زینهار is the admonition and به جان is a dative/idiomatic phrase specifying the purpose ('for life' / 'to save life'). Together the clause means 'I did not give anyone the warning (that might save their life)'.
segments.s403-g28: گیا 'plant/herb' is the subject; در بیابان sets the location ('in the desert'). سرآورد is a classical past-form meaning 'brought forth/produced' and takes بار ('load/fruit/produce') as its object — here 'بار' = produce/fruit, so the clause says 'a plant in the desert bore fruit.'
segments.s403-g29: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/always' placed before the verb phrase for emphasis. مغز مردم is a genitive construction 'the people's brains' (noun before possessor in this poetic order). خورد is the past form of خوردن 'to eat/consume' and, with poetic inversion, the subjects شیر و گرگ ('lion and wolf') appear after the verb; the line describes habitual predation ('the lion and the wolf eat the people's brains').
segments.s403-g30: جز از دل introduces an exclusive phrase 'except/from the heart'; نجوید here functions as a classical present ('seeks' / 'desires') with the subject placed after the verb (poetic inversion). پلنگ سترگ is the subject plus epithet — the line therefore means 'only the great panther seeks the heart.'
segments.s403-g31: فلک روشن is a predicate-adjective phrase ('the sky bright'); از introduces the causal/agentive phrase 'by/through/because of' تاج گشتاسپ ('Gushtasp's crown'). باد at the end is the optative particle 'may it be' (a common classical wish formula). The order is poetic but reads 'May the sky be bright because of Gushtasp's crown.'
segments.s403-g32: زمین گلشن reads 'the earth [be] a garden' with گلشن as predicate; شاه لهراسپ specifies for whom/which the earth should be a garden ('King Lohrasp'). باد again is the optative 'may it be', giving the whole line the sense of a blessing/wish for prosperity under Lohrasp.
segments.s403-g41: سر here means the heading/top (not anatomical head) and forms the phrase 'سر پاسخ‌نامه' = 'the heading of the reply'. بود is the past copula 'was'. از نخست is a prepositional phrase 'from the beginning/at first' (از + نخست), giving temporal framing.
segments.s403-g42: که introduces the content/relative clause (here introducing the quoted wish). پاینده is an adjective 'everlasting'. بادآنک is the fused optative construction باد + آن‌که (classical 'may he who…'); the supplied lexicon sense doesn't match this grammatical function, so a specific optative reading is required. نیکی بجست = 'who sought goodness' (بجست is past 'sought', relative to آن‌که).
segments.s403-g43: خرد یافته is a compound past-participial adjective 'having acquired wisdom' (i.e., 'wise') that precedes the noun; it modifies مرد, yielding 'the wise man'. یزدان‌شناس is a compound noun 'God-knower' / 'pious person' that further identifies the man.
segments.s403-g44: به نیکی = 'for/in respect of the goodness' (به + noun). ز یزدان = 'from God' (ز as poetic 'from' + یزدان). شناسد here functions as a verbal/nominal root meaning recognition/knowing (classical present form), and سپاس is 'thanks' (here used in the sense 'gave thanks' in an elliptical classical construction).
segments.s403-g45: دگر 'another' identifies a different speaker. گفت is the verb 'said' (the supplied lexicon sense is conversational noun-like and does not match the verbal use here). کز = classical 'that/from which' introducing what is being sought; دادگر is the adjective 'just' modifying خدای 'God', with یک 'one' emphasizing unity.
segments.s403-g46: بخواهیم is first-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'let us ask / may we ask' (classical desire). کو here functions archaically as a relative 'who/that' (not the usual 'where') introducing the clause about the God we ask for. باشدت is an optative/jussive form meaning 'may he be (to you)' or 'may he become' (classical -د ending); رهنمای is the predicate noun 'guide'.
segments.s403-g47: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree'. بکشتم is an archaic 1st-person past verb 'I planted/caused to grow' (classical formation). به باغ بهشت is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the garden of Paradise' (به + باغ + بهشت).
segments.s403-g48: کزان = 'from which' (کَه + ز/زان) links the relative clause to the tree. بارورتر is the comparative adjective 'more fruitful/more productive' (تر comparative suffix). فریدون is the proper name (the legendary king). نکشت here is classical past with negation (ن + کشت) meaning 'did not plant' — the supplied lexicon senses do not reflect this verbal/negative past, so the lexical match is absent.
segments.s403-g9: A paratactic epithet: خداوند (title 'lord') followed by two nouns joined by و. There is no copula — this is a stacked list of attributes: 'lord of X and Y'. فرهی is an attributive noun/adjective ('noble, of glory') linked by و to پیروزی.
segments.s403-g10: Another coordinate epithet beginning again with خداوند. دیهیم is an adjectival/relational word 'of the villages / rural', و connects it to شاهنشهی 'kingship/imperial rule'. The segment is again nominal (no verb) and continues the catalogue of titles.
segments.s403-g11: Two coordinated epithets introduced by repeated خداوند: 'lord of جان (life/soul)' and 'lord of رای (counsel/plan)'. The repetition of خداوند is a poetic device listing distinct lordships; there is no verb or copula.
segments.s403-g12: Continues the list: خداوند + compound/participial adjective نیکی‌ده ('giver/causer of goodness') coordinated with رهنمای ('guide'). The -ده element marks the agentive/causal sense ('one who gives'). Again nominal, no finite verb.
segments.s403-g13: ازو ('from him') introduces the beneficiary/source; جاودان کام is a noun phrase 'eternal fulfillment/satisfaction'. گشتاسپ شاد places the personal name before the adjective 'شاد' (happy) — an elliptic, optative construction where the wish is expressed without the verb (the optative is supplied by باد in the next segment).
segments.s403-g14: به مینو ('in/to heaven') gives the location; همه modifies یاد ('the remembrance/memory of everyone' or 'for all'); لهراسپ is the name in genitival relation to یاد (the thing remembered). باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)', which supplies the wished-for verb for the preceding clauses — a common poetic construction.
segments.s403-g15: رسیدم is 1st-person past 'I arrived / I came'. It is followed by directional/locative phrases introduced by به: به راهی (onto/a road) and به توران زمین (into the land of Turan). The order is verb then prepositional phrases describing where the speaker arrived.
segments.s403-g16: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'راهی'. هرگز نخوانم is negative first-person ('I shall never read/sing/recite'). برو آفرین is an archaic/elliptical phrase: read as an inverted or clipped form of آفرین بر او ('praise be to/for it/him'), so together the clause means 'which I will never sing/praise'. The construction omits an explicit object because آفرین (acclamation/praise) and the preposition are compacted in poetic diction.
segments.s403-g49: برش سرخ is a noun + adjective phrase ('its red face/appearance'); یاقوت و زر are coordinated nouns ('ruby and gold') that form the predicate with آمدست. آمدست is an archaic perfect/preterite-copular verb here meaning 'has appeared/has come into being.' Word order places the descriptive noun phrase before the predicate, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s403-g50: همه برگ او = 'all his/its leaves' (noun + pronominal possession). زیب و فر are two abstract nouns ('beauty' and 'royal glory') forming a compound predicate with آمدست again used as 'have come / manifest themselves.' The structure means 'all its leaves are (full of) beauty and splendour.'
segments.s403-g51: بماناد is an optative/archaic 3rd-person form meaning 'may it remain.' تا جاودان gives the duration/extent 'until (be) eternal / forever.' این درخت is the noun phrase that is the patient/subject of the wish; adjective (جاودان) precedes the demonstrative and noun for poetic emphasis.
segments.s403-g52: This is a conventional optative formula: ترا باد = 'may it be to you' (ترا = 'to you' as enclitic, را serving the clitic form) with باد here as the optative particle 'may.' شادان دل و نیک‌بخت are predicative adjectives/nouns ('joyful-hearted and fortunate') describing the wished-for state of the addressee.
segments.s403-g53: یکی آنک گفتی که is a relative/introduction: 'one (a certain one) that you said...' آنک functions as a relative connector ('which/who'); گفتی here is the reported-speech verb 'you said' (the provided lexical senses do not include the verbal reading, so see note). کین نیا is a compound meaning 'ancestral hatred/vengeance' (kīn + nīyā).
segments.s403-g54: بجستم is the main verb in the clause (here read as a past, 'I sought/endeavoured to'), but its exact etymology is obscure in the supplied lexicon (hence uncertainty). پر از چاره و کیمیا is a prepositional/adjectival phrase 'full of devices/means and alchemy' that modifies the verb phrase, so the whole segment reads 'I sought (or strove), full of stratagems and alchemical craft.'
segments.s403-g55: دگر آنک گفتی = 'the other (one) that you mentioned'; ز خون ریختن is a gerundial/infinitive phrase 'from/for shedding blood' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'in regard to' and خون ریختن is the action 'to shed blood' (here 'bloodshed').
segments.s403-g56: به تنها به رزم اندر آویختن is a clustered verbal phrase: به تنها ('alone') and به رزم ('to/into battle') are prepositional phrases modifying آویختن, and اندر adds a locative sense ('in/into'). آویختن here means 'to cling to/attach oneself (to)' and so the whole means 'to throw oneself into battle alone' or 'to take to fighting alone.'
segments.s403-g73: همیشه (adverb) 'always' sets the general wish. بدی here names adversity/badness (an abstract noun) and is paired with the adjective شاد 'joyful', an idiomatic wish 'be joyful in adversity'. و connects the two spheres (adversity and the next phrase). به روزگار is a prepositional phrase literally 'to/at the times' = 'in (one's) fortunes or in days (both good and bad)'; word order is poetic and compressed but reads as two conditions in which the addressee is wished to remain joyful.
segments.s403-g74: روان را places 'the soul' as the affected/beneficiary (را is the object/definiteness marker attached to روان). خرد is the noun 'wisdom' which functions as the thing to be yielded. بادت is an optative/formulaic verb (باد + enclitic) meaning 'may it be to you' or 'may it be (for you)' — an old poetic way to express a wish. آموزگار is the predicate noun 'teacher' (i.e. 'may wisdom be a teacher to your soul'). The clause order (object — noun — optative — predicate) is inverted for poetic effect: literally 'to the soul wisdom may be a teacher (to you)'.
segments.s403-g75: نیازست is an impersonal/auxiliary predicate 'it is necessary' (or 'we need'); ما را follows as the dative/beneficiary 'for us' (in older Persian constructions the person comes after نیازست marked by را). به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase 'to a meeting with you' or 'to see you' (به + noun + تو). The whole clause means 'It is necessary for us to see you' / 'We need to see you.'
segments.s403-g76: This clause begins with the imperative/command بدان = ب‑ (prefix) + دان (stem 'know') meaning 'know/be aware'. پر خرد is an adjectival phrase 'full of wisdom' (پر = 'full (of)', خرد = 'wisdom') modifying جان 'soul'. بیدار is the adjective 'awake/alert'. تو at the end is the possessor 'your'. The sequence thus says 'Know that your soul, full of wisdom, is awake' (بدان introduces the propositional content that follows).
segments.s403-g77: چه ... بخوانی is a free relative/subjunctive pattern: چه + present/subjunctive verb expresses 'whatever you may read' or 'whatsoever letter you read'. نامه is the object 'letter'. بنه here is a poetic imperative 'place/put/lay (down)' (an older verbal form not represented by the noun senses given in the list), followed by بر نشـان 'on/upon (as) a sign/token' (بر = on, نشان = sign/token). So the clause orders an action: 'Whatever letter you read, place it upon the token/mark.'
segments.s403-g78: بدین is a deictic prepositional phrase 'to this' (ب‑ + این in classical spelling) marking direction; بارگاه is 'the royal court/palace' (destination). آی is the imperative 'come!' The following با + سرکشان means 'with the سرکشان' — 'with the chiefs/nobles/leading men' (سرکشان is a plural; the lemma senses given do not match this usage and so are irregular here). The whole clause is an imperative: 'Come to this court with the chiefs.'
segments.s403-g79: هیون تگاور is a compound/name: a ferocious creature/raider (هیون = savage beast/monster) qualified by تگاور (a warrior/raider/rider). ز is the classical/prepositional 'from'. در here means 'gate' (rather than 'in'), and بازگشت is the verbal noun 'return' (i.e. 'on returning' or 'when he returned'). Together the phrase gives the temporal/ablative clause 'when/to/after Hyun the raider returned from the gate.'
segments.s403-g80: همه شهر ایران is the subject phrase 'the whole city (or all the city) of Iran' (همه = all/whole, شهر = city, ایران = Iran). پرآواز is a compound adjective meaning 'full-voiced / resounding / loudly famed' and گشت is the past of گشتن used here as 'became' (i.e. 'grew/become'). The predicate 'پرآواز گشت' therefore means 'became resounding/noisy' or 'became celebrated'.
segments.s403-g57: تن شهریاران گرامی بود is a copular clause: تن (here meaning 'person/self') is the noun, شهریاران is a genitive (the rulers') modifying it, گرامی is the adjective 'honoured/esteemed' that follows the noun, and بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is Noun + Genitive + Adjective + Copula (classical/poetic Persian).
segments.s403-g58: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. از کوشش سخت is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/through hard effort' (از here = 'by/from' in a literary sense), کوشش = 'effort', سخت = 'severe/hard'. نامی بود = 'he was famed' (نامی = 'famous', بود = past copula). The clause modifies the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s403-g59: نگهدار تن باش is an imperative construction: نگهدار functions as 'guardian/keeper' (here addressed as a role to assume), تن = 'the body', and باش is the 2nd-person imperative 'be'. The conjunction و links the next noun phrase (آن خرد) under the same injunction: 'and that wisdom'.
segments.s403-g60: که introduces a relative clause modifying آن خرد: 'which/that'. جان را is the definite object 'the soul' (را marking it). به دانش خرد is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/through the knowledge (of) wisdom' (به here = 'by/through' in literary use). پرورد is a past form 'nurtured/raised' (3rd singular) describing what that wisdom did to the soul.
segments.s403-g61: سه دیگر = 'three others' (numeral + 'other'). که introduces reported speech or specification ('that/which'). گفتی here contextually is the 2nd-person past 'you said', followed by the oath-form به جان زینهار ('by my life, take heed' — زینهار is an admonition/imperative). Note: the supplied senses list for گفتی did not include the verbal past 'you said', so the sense index for that word is marked -1.
segments.s403-g62: ندادم is negative past 1st-person 'I did not give' (ن- prefix + دادم). کسی را = 'to anyone' (کسی 'someone/anyone', را object marker). ز چندان سوار = 'of/from so many horsemen' where ز is the poetic form of از ('from/of' here = 'so many' as measure), چندان = 'that much/so many', and سوار = 'rider/horseman'. Together: 'I did not give so many horsemen to anyone.'
segments.s403-g63: همیشه (adverb) 'always' sets the temporal frame. دلت is 'your heart' (دل + 2nd-person suffix -ت). مهربان باد is an optative/wish: باد = 'may it be' and مهربان = 'kind'; و گرم adds a coordinated adjective 'and warm'. The construction is a conventional optative blessing 'May your heart always be kind and warm.'
segments.s403-g64: This is a pair of small noun phrases describing desirable qualities: پر از شرم = 'full of modesty' (پر 'full (of)' + از + شرم 'modesty/shame' used here as modesty), جان here refers to the soul/life (addressing the inner self) andلب پر آوای نرم links 'lip(s) full of gentle melody' (لب 'lip', پر 'full', آوای 'melody/voice', نرم 'soft/gentle'). Together the sense is: 'may (your heart/soul) be full of modesty and may your lips be filled with a gentle song.'
segments.s403-g65: مبادا is an optative/prohibitive particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'do not'; ترا is the object 'you' written as the enclitic -ت plus the object marker را (here functioning together as 'you'/'your'); پیشه + [infinitive] (پیشه خون ریختن) is a nominal construction where پیشه 'profession/trade' takes the infinitive ریختن to mean 'the practice of spilling/shedding blood.' Word order is typical Persian: particle + object + noun phrase with an infinitive complement.
segments.s403-g66: نه is the coordinating negative 'nor' linking this clause to the previous prohibition; بی‌کینه is an adverbial phrase 'without malice/ill-will' modifying the following verbal noun; با مهتر آویختن is a prepositional phrase where با + مهتر ('with/to a chief, elder') takes the infinitive آویختن 'to cling/attach oneself' used as a noun. The infinitive (آویختن) is the head of the action being proscribed.
segments.s403-g67: به کین is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/for (the) hatred' (به here indicates the cause). برادرت is 'your brother' with the 2nd-person enclitic -ت. بی سی و هشت appears as a numeral phrase in old/poetic spacing (the orthography is non‑modern) and functions as an appositive or descriptive number attached to برادرت; the exact modern reading is ambiguous from the spacing but it is a numeral phrase.
segments.s403-g68: از اندازه is a causal/agentive phrase 'from (the) measure' used idiomatically here to mean 'from excess' or 'because of too great an amount'; خون ریختن is again the verbal-noun 'shedding blood'; درگذشت is the past tense 'he passed away / died.' The clause states the cause (از اندازه خون ریختن) followed by the result (درگذشت).
segments.s403-g69: و is the coordinating 'and'; دیگر means 'other/others' or 'another'; کزان is a relative/prepositional compound 'from which/of whom' (ک + ز + آن); پیر گشته is a perfect participial phrase 'having become old / the elder having become (so)'; نیا here is lexically ambiguous in isolation (it can be a noun 'ancestor' or a verbal/imperative form in other contexts) and in classical lines often participates in compacted expressions — the construction overall forms a relative clause 'and others, from whom the elder had ...'.
segments.s403-g70: ز دل دور کرده is a causative/perfect construction: ز (from) + دل (the heart) + دور کرده (lit. 'made distant' or 'driven away', کرد + ه perfect participle), meaning 'having put / kept away from the heart.' بد و کیمیا are two nouns joined by و 'and' (here 'evil' and 'kīmiyā'); the phrase states what was driven away from the heart. Note که کرده is a compound participle functioning as a verbal adjective.
segments.s403-g71: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when.' خون ریختندش is a past-tense verb phrase: ریختند (they poured/they shed) plus the clitic -ش (him/it), so 'they shed his blood.' تو is the emphatic subject 'you'; خون ریختی is the past 2nd-person singular 'you shed blood.' The clause juxtaposes a third‑person past action with a second‑person past action.
segments.s403-g72: چو again 'when/like'; شیران جنگی is a simile/noun phrase 'war‑like lions' where شیران (plural) can be literal 'lions' or metaphorical 'brave men/warriors' and جنگی qualifies them as 'warlike' or 'fighters'; برآویختی is a past 2nd‑person singular verb 'you sprang up/rose up' (classical poetic verb meaning 'to rise up/engage'). The phrase describes the manner of the subject's rising — like warlike lions.

sentences.s403-s17: When they set Esfandiyar's seal on the courier and sought a few horsemen.
sentences.s403-s18: They dispatched to Iran foaming, swift beasts under a new commander.
sentences.s403-s19: He pursued after the reply‑letter; the fire slew the ill‑willed man.
sentences.s403-s20: Little came of it until the answer arrived; someone unlocked the badly bound, ill‑fated letter with a key.
sentences.s403-s41: When the Hyunan horsemen returned, they rode up to Tahmatan's presence.
sentences.s403-s1: He summoned the worldly‑wise scribe before him; with that plan he struck/plucked the harp for a little while.
sentences.s403-s2: The fortunate scribe sat upon the throne; he asked for a pen, paper, musk and perfume.
sentences.s403-s3: As soon as the tip of the pen became black, he exclaimed, 'Praise be to the Lord Moon.'
sentences.s403-s4: The lord Kiwan and Nahid and Hoor; the lord of the elephant and the lord of the ant.
sentences.s403-s9: If I reveal the whole tale, a man's head will be made new from ancient sorrow.
sentences.s403-s10: What command have you for me, O king? Shall I read the letter of the campaign?
sentences.s403-s11: At meeting him I shall become joyful and glad; from this long‑standing suffering I shall be free of sorrow.
sentences.s403-s12: And from those remedies that I devised, so that I might rid the heart of hatred.
sentences.s403-s13: At Arjasp's iron citadel and at Kahram there remained nothing but wailing, pain, and mourning.
sentences.s403-s14: I gave no one the warning to save their life; a plant in the desert bore fruit.
sentences.s403-s15: Lions and wolves keep devouring the people's brains; only the mighty panther seeks the heart.
sentences.s403-s16: May the heavens be bright with Gushtasp's crown, and may the earth be a garden for King Lohrasp.
sentences.s403-s21: The heading of the reply was, from the start: "May he be everlasting who sought goodness."
sentences.s403-s22: The wise, God-knowing man, out of gratitude for the good from God, gave thanks.
sentences.s403-s23: Another said: 'Let us ask of the just one a single God who may be your guide.'
sentences.s403-s24: I planted a tree in the garden of Paradise, from which none more fruitful did Fereydun plant.
sentences.s403-s5: Lord of victory and of nobility; lord of the villages and of imperial kingship.
sentences.s403-s6: Lord of life and lord of counsel; lord who gives goodness and guide.
sentences.s403-s7: May eternal fulfillment come from him; may Gushtasp be glad; may Lohrasp’s remembrance be in heaven for all.
sentences.s403-s8: I came upon a road into the land of Turan that I will never sing praises of.
sentences.s403-s25: Its red face came forth in ruby and gold; all its leaves are beauty and royal splendour.
sentences.s403-s26: May this tree remain eternal; may you be of joyful heart and fortunate.
sentences.s403-s27: One — the one you mentioned — that I sought (or strove against) this ancestral grudge, full of stratagems and alchemical art.
sentences.s403-s28: The other — the one you mentioned — was for shedding blood and, alone, took to engaging in battle.
sentences.s403-s37: May you always be joyful in both bad times and good fortune, and may the vicissitudes of time be a teacher of wisdom to your soul.
sentences.s403-s38: It is necessary for us to see you; know that your soul, full of wisdom, is awake.
sentences.s403-s39: Whatever letter you may read, set it down as a token, and come to this court with the chiefs.
sentences.s403-s40: When the savage raider Hyun returned through the gate, the whole city of Iran grew loud (was filled with cries).
sentences.s403-s29: The person of kings was honoured, for he was famed through severe exertion.
sentences.s403-s30: Be the guardian of the body, and (be) of that wisdom which, by knowledge, nurtured the soul.
sentences.s403-s31: As for three others — those you said 'by my life, take heed' — I did not give so many horsemen to anyone.
sentences.s403-s32: May your heart always be kind and warm, and may your lips be full of modesty and soft song.
sentences.s403-s33: May your trade never be the shedding of blood, nor the clinging to a chief without malice.
sentences.s403-s34: Out of hatred, your brother — thirty-eight — died from shedding blood beyond measure.
sentences.s403-s35: And others — from whom the elder had gone — had driven evil and the elixir far from the heart.
sentences.s403-s36: When they shed his blood, you shed blood; when you sprang up like warlike lions.
